title: Soft light: map input to brightness
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Wire a potentiometer to one pin and an LED to another, upload the Arduino sketch, and turn the knob to set the LED's brightness. Then see the mechanism underneath — the knob is a voltage divider that splits 3.3V into a value the board reads, and that value becomes the light, live. One unbroken loop, from your hand to the glow.

sourceMetadata.imageAlt: Official Freenove circuit for Soft Light: a potentiometer with its outer pins on 3.3V and ground and its middle pin on GPIO 1, and an LED on GPIO 14 through a 220 ohm resistor to ground, shown as both a schematic and a breadboard photo.
sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: GPIO extension board
parts.explanation: GPIO means general-purpose input/output. These pins are connection points your code can use. The extension board makes them easier to see, reach, and wire without cramming everything onto the ESP32-S3 itself.

parts.name: Rotary potentiometer
parts.explanation: A potentiometer is a resistor with a third pin on a movable contact. Turning the shaft slides that contact along the resistor, so the voltage on the middle pin sweeps smoothly between the two ends. Wire the ends to 3.3V and ground and the knob becomes a voltage dial your ADC pin can read.

parts.name: LED
parts.explanation: A LED is a one-way light. It only lights when its longer leg (the +) faces the pin and its shorter leg (the −) faces ground. Too much current burns it out, so a LED always sits behind a resistor.

parts.name: 220 Ω resistor
parts.explanation: A resistor is a small component whose whole job is to limit how much current flows through a wire. Current is the flow of electricity through the circuit; a resistor throttles that flow down to a safe amount. Its strength is measured in ohms (Ω) — a larger number resists harder and lets less current through. A LED wired straight to power pulls far more current than it can survive and burns out at once, so it always sits in series with a resistor. The 220 Ω value here holds the current gentle enough to light the LED and keep it alive. The coloured bands print the resistor's value, and it works the same either way round — a resistor has no positive or negative end.

parts.name: 5 jumper wires
parts.explanation: Jumper wires make temporary connections without soldering. Female-to-male wires have a socket on one end and a pin on the other, useful when a sensor module and board use different connector styles.

wiring.reason: The divider's midpoint — the board reads this voltage as a number.
Sets the top of the knob's voltage divider at 3.3V.
Sets the bottom of the divider at 0V.
This pin shades the LED with PWM.
Completes the LED's path back to zero volts.

coachInstructions: Guide one physical connection at a time and wait for learner confirmation before proceeding.
Teach the voltage divider as the core idea — the knob is two resistances across 3.3V and ground, the wiper taps the midpoint, and turning it slides that midpoint voltage between 0 and 3.3V.
The pot's middle pin goes to GPIO 1. One page of the manual's prose names a different pin; the code uses GPIO 1 and the code is truth — always route the middle pin to GPIO 1.
If the LED is stuck at full or stuck dark at every position, suspect the middle-pin wire on GPIO 1 first.
If turning the knob changes nothing at all, check the outer pot pins on 3.3V and GND — the two ends of the divider.
If the LED never lights, check its direction, the 220 Ω resistor, and the GPIO 14 wire before changing code.
For the Serial challenge, have them add Serial.begin(115200) and Serial.println(adcVal), watch the reading sweep 0 to 4095, then flip the mapping and predict which end goes dark.
If asked why there's no map() call, explain that 12-bit PWM matches the 12-bit ADC so the raw reading is already a valid duty; the MicroPython path remaps to 0–1023 and its numbers will differ.
If they ask how a real sensor fits in, explain that the same divider with a photoresistor or thermistor in place of the knob moves on its own as light or heat changes.
steps: Seat the ESP32-S3 on the GPIO extension board and keep USB unplugged while you wire.
Stand the potentiometer on the breadboard so each of its three pins lands in its own row.
Wire one outer pot pin to 3.3V and the other outer pin to GND.
Run the pot's middle pin — the divider's midpoint — to GPIO 1, the wire the board reads.
Place the LED so its long leg (+) is on the GPIO 14 side and its short leg (−) heads toward ground.
Put the 220 Ω resistor in series between GPIO 14 and the LED's long leg.
Compare every wire to the chart before you plug in USB.
Open Sketch_10.1_SoftLight.ino in Arduino IDE and upload it.
Turn the knob slowly from end to end and watch the LED follow your hand.

arduino.explanation: Sets up PWM on GPIO 14 at 12-bit resolution, so brightness runs 0 to 4095 — the same span the ADC produces.
Reads the knob's voltage on GPIO 1 as a number from 0 to 4095.
Sets the LED's duty to that number. The two scales match, so the raw reading passes straight through as a brightness — the "re-map" comment overstates what this line does.

micropython.explanation: Sets the ADC to 12 bits so readings run 0 to 4095 — the same span the Arduino sketch reads.
MicroPython's duty here is 10-bit, so the reading is squeezed from 0–4095 down to 0–1023 before it drives the LED.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: The knob is a voltage divider; its midpoint voltage becomes an ADC number, the number becomes a PWM duty, and the duty becomes brightness — one control loop, run many times a second.
theoryModel.formula: voltage divider → ADC number → PWM duty → brightness

notes.title: Why the knob is a voltage divider
notes.body: Two resistances in series split the supply at their meeting point in proportion to their sizes. The knob makes that split adjustable — the wiper moves the boundary between the upper stretch of track and the lower one, so the midpoint voltage moves with your wrist.

notes.title: The same divider, without a hand
notes.body: Replace one half of the divider with a photoresistor and the split shifts on its own as the light changes — no knob to turn. That is how most of the kit's sensors reach the ADC, which is why this one pattern is worth knowing cold.

notes.title: Why there's no map() call
notes.body: The sketch sets the PWM to 12 bits on purpose. Both scales run 0 to 4095, so the ADC reading is already a valid duty and passes through untouched. When the scales differ — as in the MicroPython version — you remap first.

expectedOutputExample: Turn the knob to one end — the LED sits dark.
Turn it to the other end — the LED reaches full brightness.
Everywhere in between, the brightness follows the knob smoothly, with no jumps or dead spots.
test.successCriteria: Which end is dark depends on which outer pin you wired to GND — either way round is a working circuit.

troubleshooting.symptom: LED stuck at full or stuck dark
firstChecks: Check the pot's middle-pin wire — it must reach GPIO 1.
Check the outer pot pins — one to 3.3V, one to GND.

troubleshooting.symptom: LED dark at every knob position
firstChecks: Check the LED's direction and the 220 Ω resistor on the GPIO 14 side.
Swap in a data-capable USB cable.

challenge: Print the raw ADC value to the Serial Monitor, watch it sweep 0 to 4095 as you turn the knob, then use what you see to predict and flip the mapping direction.
